items.q: What property of a sound wave determines its pitch?
items.correct: The frequency — higher frequency means higher pitch.
items.misconception: Amplitude determines pitch — a bigger wave is a higher-pitched note.
items.random_wrong: The speed of the wave determines pitch — faster waves sound higher.
items.why: Students conflate the two main wave properties: amplitude relates to volume, frequency to pitch, but they often swap them.

items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: What is the difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
items.correct: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller ones; fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one.
items.misconception: Fission and fusion are the same process, just at different scales.
items.random_wrong: Fission combines small atoms into bigger ones, and fusion splits them.
items.why: The words sound similar (both start 'fi-'), and students mix up which one combines and which one splits.
Correct answer: -273.15°C. “-100°C” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If c for water is 4180 J/kg/K, energy to heat 2 kg by 10 K:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 83600 J. “8360 J” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: c_steel = 450 J/kg/K. Energy to heat 0.5 kg by 80 K in kJ:
items.why: Exact answer: 18. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
